math_problem: You are given a $10 \times 2$ grid of unit squares. Two different squares are adjacent if they share a side. How many ways can one mark exactly nine of the squares so that no two marked squares are adjacent? Instead of counting the total number of ways to mark exactly nine of the squares so that no two marked squares are adjacent, simply find $w$, where $w$ is the number of ways to mark one square in each of nine consecutive rows such that no two marked squares in adjacent rows share the same column. Suppose that $m$ and $n$ are positive integers with $m<n$ such that the interval $[m, n)$ contains more multiples of $(w + 2019)$ than multiples of $(w + 1998)$. Compute the maximum possible value of $n-m$.
reasoning_content:
The value of \(w\) is 2, derived from counting the number of ways to mark one square in each of nine consecutive rows of a \(10 \times 2\) grid such that no two marked squares in adjacent rows share the same column. This results in two possible patterns (starting with either column).

Given \(w = 2\), we have \(w + 2019 = 2021\) and \(w + 1998 = 2000\). The problem reduces to finding the maximum possible length \(L = n - m\) of an interval \([m, n)\) such that there are more multiples of 2021 than multiples of 2000 in that interval.

The maximum \(L\) is achieved when \(L = 191999\), corresponding to the largest integer satisfying the necessary condition \(\left\lfloor\frac{L-1}{2021}\right\rfloor \ge \left\lfloor\frac{L}{2000}\right\rfloor\). This occurs for \(k = 95\), yielding \(L = (95+1) \times 2000 - 1 = 191999\). An explicit construction with \(m = q \times 2000 + 1\) for an appropriate \(q\) confirms achievability.

{  
    "reasoning": "The value of w is 2 because marking one square in each of nine consecutive rows with the given condition forces an alternating pattern, determined by the choice of column for the first row. With w = 2, we set a = 2021 and b = 2000. The maximum interval length L = n - m such that multiples of a outnumber multiples of b in [m, n) is found by maximizing L subject to floor((L-1)/a) ≥ floor(L/b). The largest L satisfying this is (k+1)b - 1 where k = floor(L/b) and (k+1)b - a*k ≥ 2. Solving 2000 - 21k ≥ 2 gives k ≤ 95, so L_max = 96*2000 - 1 = 191999. An explicit construction verifies this length is achievable.",  
    "answer": "191999"  
}

A1402: Mathematics -> Applied Mathematics -> Statistics -> Probability -> Counting Methods -> Combinations
